original_hypothesis.title: claimDeposits Breaks Conservation Invariant by Not Decrementing totalDeposits
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: When users claim refunds via claimDeposits, userDeposits[msg.sender] is set to 0 but totalDeposits is never decremented. This breaks the conservation invariant totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits). While the pool is NOT_QUALIFIED (dead-end state), any code path that reads totalDeposits after claims would use a stale inflated value.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a demonstrable code defect. totalDeposits is incremented in depositToken but never decremented in claimDeposits. The asymmetric accounting could affect balanceOf or deductAmount if the pool state is somehow manipulated back to an active state.

proof_of_signal.pattern: claimDeposits sets userDeposits[msg.sender]=0 but totalDeposits is not decremented; compare with depositToken at lines 124-142 which increments both

preconditions: Users have non-zero deposits to claim

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: depositToken: totalDeposits += _amount and userDeposits[spender] += _amount; claimDeposits: userDeposits[msg.sender] = 0 but totalDeposits unchanged
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: After claims, totalDeposits > sum(userDeposits), creating stale state
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Code defect with potential downstream impact if pool state transitions back to active

economic_questions: Can the stale totalDeposits value be exploited if pool transitions out of NOT_QUALIFIED?
Does the ECON-H002 reset path create a scenario where stale totalDeposits matters?
validation_requirements: Verify totalDeposits is not modified in claimDeposits
Check if combined with pool reset (ECON-H002), stale totalDeposits causes further damage

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Indirect - enables further exploitation if combined with state manipulation

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Future users if pool is reset
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Depends on whether state can be reactivated

investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace all reads of totalDeposits to assess downstream impact

validation_result.impact_summary: claimDeposits sets userDeposits[msg.sender]=0 but never decrements totalDeposits, permanently breaking the conservation invariant totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits). While limited by NOT_QUALIFIED being normally terminal, the unchecked setPoolStatus function means stale totalDeposits could impact balanceOf/deductAmount if pool state is manipulated.
validation_result.detailed_analysis:
The hypothesis is correct. In depositToken (GenesisPool.sol lines ~138-139), both userDeposits[spender] and totalDeposits are incremented symmetrically. However, in claimDeposits (lines ~196-204), userDeposits[msg.sender] is set to 0 but totalDeposits is never modified. After all users claim their refunds, totalDeposits retains its original value while sum(userDeposits) becomes 0.

This is definitively a code defect - the accounting is asymmetric. The variable totalDeposits is used as a denominator in balanceOf() and as a multiplier in deductAmount(). While in the NOT_QUALIFIED state (when claimDeposits is callable), liquidity==0 prevents immediate exploitation through balanceOf/deductAmount, the invariant violation is real.

Critically, setPoolStatus() (line ~183) has NO state transition validation - any status can be set from any state by the manager. Combined with this accounting bug, if a pool were somehow re-activated after claims, totalDeposits would be inflated relative to actual userDeposits, causing incorrect share calculations in balanceOf and deductAmount.

Notably, deductAmount and deductAllAmount also reduce userDeposits without decrementing totalDeposits, but this is intentional for the LAUNCHED state where totalDeposits serves as a fixed proportional denominator. The claimDeposits case is fundamentally different because it represents actual refund of deposits, not proportional unstaking.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code evidence confirms the defect. depositToken increments both variables (lines 138-139), but claimDeposits only zeros userDeposits (lines 198-199) without touching totalDeposits. No other function ever decrements totalDeposits.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: Both userDeposits and totalDeposits are incremented symmetrically during deposit.
userDeposits[msg.sender] is zeroed but totalDeposits is never decremented, breaking the conservation invariant.
Uses totalDeposits as denominator. If pool were re-activated after claims, the stale inflated totalDeposits would cause incorrect balance calculations.
No state transition validation - any status can be set from any state, meaning a pool could theoretically transition from NOT_QUALIFIED back to an active state where stale totalDeposits would matter.

calling_sequence: 1. Pool reaches NOT_QUALIFIED status (via rejectPool or setPoolStatus from manager)
2. User calls claimDeposits() - passes poolStatus == NOT_QUALIFIED check
4. userDeposits[msg.sender] = 0 zeros the user's individual record
5. Token transfer occurs, refunding the user
6. totalDeposits remains at its original accumulated value - NEVER decremented
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: After step 6, the invariant totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits[all]) is broken. For N users who each deposited D tokens, after all claims: sum(userDeposits) = 0 but totalDeposits = N*D. This stale value persists in contract storage. If setPoolStatus (line 183, no transition validation) were called to set pool back to an active status, balanceOf() at line 235 would divide by the inflated totalDeposits, computing incorrect (reduced) balances for any users.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for GenesisPool specifically. The defect is inherent in the contract code regardless of configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: In normal operation, NOT_QUALIFIED is a terminal state and the stale totalDeposits has no immediate exploitable impact since liquidity==0 prevents balanceOf from returning non-zero values. However, the defect is confirmed as a code bug: the accounting is provably asymmetric. The risk is elevated by the fact that setPoolStatus has no state transition validation (INV-STATE-005), meaning a compromised or buggy manager could theoretically re-activate a pool after claims have been processed, at which point the stale totalDeposits would cause incorrect proportional calculations in balanceOf and deductAmount.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The conservation invariant totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits) is definitively broken after any claimDeposits call. In isolation (normal flow), the impact is limited because NOT_QUALIFIED is terminal and liquidity==0. However, combined with the lack of state transition validation in setPoolStatus (a separate but related defect), the stale totalDeposits could lead to incorrect share calculations if the pool were re-activated. This is a demonstrable code defect regardless of exploitability.
recommended_mitigations: Add `totalDeposits -= _amount;` in claimDeposits() after `userDeposits[msg.sender] = 0;` to maintain the conservation invariant
Add state transition validation in setPoolStatus() to prevent backward transitions from NOT_QUALIFIED
Consider adding an invariant check function that verifies totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits) for testing purposes
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy GenesisPool with test tokens. 2. Via manager, set pool to PRE_LISTING and have users deposit tokens (observe totalDeposits increments). 3. Set pool to NOT_QUALIFIED via setPoolStatus. 4. Have all users call claimDeposits(). 5. Read totalDeposits (still original value) vs sum of userDeposits (0). 6. Optionally, call setPoolStatus to re-activate pool and verify balanceOf returns incorrect values.
uncertainties: Whether setPoolStatus can be called to transition from NOT_QUALIFIED back to active in production (depends on manager implementation and governance controls)
Whether the intentional non-decrement in deductAmount/deductAllAmount was meant to also apply to claimDeposits (possible design confusion)
additional_testing_needed: Fuzz test: deposit various amounts, claim deposits, verify totalDeposits vs sum(userDeposits)
Integration test: attempt to re-activate pool after claims and verify balanceOf behavior
Invariant test: assert totalDeposits == sum(userDeposits[all depositors]) at all state transitions
